Syrian Billionaires Needed a Favor in Washington. They Invoked the Trump Name.
The Trump family has financial ties to Middle Eastern business executives who stood to benefit after President Trump signed a law repealing sanctions against Syria.
Jared Kushner is a business partner with the Syrian-born, Qatar-based family that has secured $12 billion in Syrian reconstruction deals — projects that needed the sanctions to be repealed.

An idea for a Trump International Golf Course in Syria has also been proposed, pushed in part by a Republican member of Congress looking to build support for sanctions repeal, though the Trump Organization said no such project is being considered.
The Khayyat family, which is behind the $12 billion in Syria reconstruction projects, says their financial partnership with Kushner is unrelated to the sanctions repeal effort.

But overall, this mixing of Trump family business ventures with the debate over foreign government sanctions illustrates the unprecedented blurring of lines between business and family affairs in Trump’s second term.
Laura Boushnak for The New York Times

With Trump Novices, Can the U.S. Win the ‘Art Olympics’?
The State Department overhauled the process for choosing an artist to represent the United States at the Venice Biennale, giving control to a woman who previously owned a pet food store.
The Venice Biennale is a competition among the world’s leading artists, and most countries rely on the expertise of top museum directors and curators. Not so for the United States this year.

The commissioner, who recently owned a pet food store, has no museum experience. The curator left the art world after an exhibit that was criticized for being racially insensitive. The artist? He’s actually based in Mexico.

Some experts have worried that America’s credibility is being undermined after the State Department dropped its independent selection process and allowed a Trump official to help her friend become commissioner.

Experts said it was the most blatant example of government interference in the U.S. Pavilion since intelligence officers helped engineer Robert Rauschenberg’s win in 1964, which put American art on the map.
Spencer Wells; Jake Naughton for The New York Times
